verses.text: Elisha promises that by tomorrow flour and barley will sell cheap at the gate; a lord scoffs, 'though the LORD make windows in heaven.' Unbelief mocks the very deliverance God offers. The prophet warns: you will see it but not eat of it.

Elisha promises that by tomorrow flour and barley will sell cheap at the gate; a lord scoffs, 'though the LORD make windows in heaven.' Unbelief mocks the very deliverance God offers. The prophet warns: you will see it but not eat of it.

Four lepers at the gate reason that staying means death, so they will risk the Syrian camp — if killed, they only die anyway. Desperation drives them to act. Sometimes the way to life is to stop sitting still and step toward the one door left open.

Four lepers at the gate reason that staying means death, so they will risk the Syrian camp — if killed, they only die anyway. Desperation drives them to act. Sometimes the way to life is to stop sitting still and step toward the one door left open.

They reach the camp and find it deserted — for God made the Syrians hear a great army and flee in terror, leaving everything. The lepers eat, drink, and hoard the spoil. God had routed the host with a sound, while His people still despaired within the walls.

They reach the camp and find it deserted — for God made the Syrians hear a great army and flee in terror, leaving everything. The lepers eat, drink, and hoard the spoil. God had routed the host with a sound, while His people still despaired within the walls.

Then conscience stirs: 'we do not well; this is a day of good tidings, and we hold our peace.' They go and tell the city. Good news withheld is a wrong to those who need it; grace received is meant to be shared, not hoarded.

Then conscience stirs: 'we do not well; this is a day of good tidings, and we hold our peace.' They go and tell the city. Good news withheld is a wrong to those who need it; grace received is meant to be shared, not hoarded.

The king suspects a Syrian trap, but scouts confirm the road is strewn with abandoned gear all the way to Jordan. The deliverance is real, not a ruse. Caution is wise, yet God's mercy proves greater than the fears that would explain it away.

The king suspects a Syrian trap, but scouts confirm the road is strewn with abandoned gear all the way to Jordan. The deliverance is real, not a ruse. Caution is wise, yet God's mercy proves greater than the fears that would explain it away.

The people pour out to plunder, flour and barley sell cheap exactly as foretold, and the scoffing lord is trampled to death in the gate — seeing the plenty but tasting none. Every word of the prophet stands. Unbelief witnesses the blessing it forfeited.

The people pour out to plunder, flour and barley sell cheap exactly as foretold, and the scoffing lord is trampled to death in the gate — seeing the plenty but tasting none. Every word of the prophet stands. Unbelief witnesses the blessing it forfeited.

The narrator repeats the fulfillment so none can miss it: as Elisha said, so it came to pass. God's word and its outcome are pressed together for emphasis. He keeps both His promises of grace and His warnings of judgment to the very letter.
